 {"id":"bd-2oah","title":"EPIC: Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (Graveyard Entry 15.4)","description":"# EPIC: Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (Graveyard Entry 15.4)

## PURPOSE
Scale WAL (Write-Ahead Log) throughput for concurrent MVCC writers by using per-core WAL buffers with epoch-based ordering. Current single WAL is a serialization bottleneck for concurrent transactions.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 15.4:
- Silo (Tu et al., SOSP 2013): per-core log buffers, epoch-based commit ordering
- Aether (Johnson et al., VLDB 2010): decoupled log buffer management, early lock release
- Key insight: WAL serialization is unnecessary if epoch ordering provides equivalent guarantees

## DESIGN

### Per-Core WAL Buffers
- Each CPU core has a dedicated WAL buffer (thread-local or core-affinity)
- Transactions write to their core's buffer without contention
- No central lock on WAL append

### Epoch-Based Ordering
- Global epoch counter advanced periodically (e.g., every 10ms or every N commits)
- Each WAL entry stamped with current epoch
- Durability guarantee: all entries in epoch E are durable before epoch E+1 begins
- Recovery: replay all entries in epoch order (entries within same epoch are commutative)

### Group Commit
- All WAL buffers for epoch E flushed together (group commit)
- Single fsync for all cores' buffers at epoch boundary
- Reduces fsync overhead from O(transactions) to O(epochs)

### Recovery
- On crash: find last complete epoch across all per-core logs
- Replay all entries up to last complete epoch
- Discard entries from incomplete epoch (they never committed)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Per-core WAL buffers eliminate contention
- [ ] Epoch-based ordering provides equivalent durability guarantees
- [ ] Group commit reduces fsync frequency
- [ ] Recovery correctly handles incomplete epochs
- [ ] Benchmark: WAL throughput scales with core count

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E WAL test (bd-2oah.4) passes
3. Parallel WAL scales linearly to 4+ cores
4. Group commit reduces fsync count by >= 50pct
5.  {"id":"bd-2oah.1","title":"Design per-core WAL buffer for concurrent MVCC writes","description":"# Design per-core WAL buffer for concurrent MVCC writes

## GOAL
Design and implement per-core WAL buffers that eliminate contention on the write-ahead log for concurrent MVCC transactions.

## DESIGN

### Buffer Structure
- Thread-local WAL buffer: Vec<WalEntry> per core
- Core affinity: use thread_local! or core_affinity crate
- Buffer capacity: configurable (default 4MB per core)
- When buffer full: flush to disk, reset

### WAL Entry Format
```rust
struct WalEntry {
    epoch: u64,
    txn_id: TxnId,
    entry_type: WalEntryType,
    payload: Vec<u8>,
    crc32c: u32,
}
```

### Concurrency
- No locks on per-core buffers (thread-local access)
- Only contention point: epoch counter increment (atomic CAS)
- Flush can happen concurrently across cores

### Integration
- Replace single WAL in ffs-mvcc with per-core buffer pool
- TxnManager routes writes to current core's buffer
- Epoch manager coordinates global epoch advancement

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Per-core buffers avoid contention (no mutex on hot path)
- [ ] Buffer management handles full/flush correctly
- [ ] Core affinity works on Linux (sched_setaffinity or equivalent)
- [ ] Unit tests: concurrent writes to separate buffers
- [ ] Benchmark: throughput vs single WAL baseline","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralReef","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:25:05.486513103Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T00:33:55.958516141Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T00:33:55.958434879Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["design","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2oah.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2oah","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:25:05.486513103Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":84,"issue_id":"bd-2oah.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-2oah.2","title":"Implement epoch-based WAL ordering","description":"# Implement epoch-based WAL ordering

## GOAL
Implement the epoch mechanism that provides ordering guarantees across per-core WAL buffers without requiring a global serialization point.

## DESIGN

### Epoch Manager
```rust
struct EpochManager {
    current_epoch: AtomicU64,
    epoch_interval: Duration,     // e.g., 10ms
    commit_threshold: u64,        // advance after N commits
}
```

### Epoch Advancement
- Advance epoch when: interval expires OR commit_threshold reached (whichever first)
- On advance: atomically increment current_epoch
- All new WAL entries stamped with new epoch
- Old epoch entries become eligible for group commit

### Durability Contract
- Transaction T in epoch E is durable after epoch E is fully flushed
- Transaction commit returns only after its epoch is flushed
- Callers waiting for durability block on epoch flush (not individual entry flush)

### Recovery Ordering
- Epochs are globally ordered
- Within an epoch, entries are commutative (no ordering required)
- Recovery: for each epoch in order, apply all entries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Epoch advances correctly (time-based and count-based triggers)
- [ ] WAL entries correctly stamped with epoch
- [ ] Durability contract holds: committed entries survive crash
- [ ] Recovery respects epoch ordering
- [ ] Unit tests: epoch advance timing, entry stamping","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralReef","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:25:16.063104850Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T00:41:13.950975111Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T00:41:13.950886515Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["epoch","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2oah.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2oah","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:25:16.063104850Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2oah.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2oah.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:29.355925517Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":85,"issue_id":"bd-2oah.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-7tn4","title":"PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)","description":"# PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)

## GOAL
Implement Proofs of Retrievability (PoR) for cryptographic verification that stored data is intact and retrievable, without reading the entire dataset.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 11.11: PoR (Juels-Kaliski 2007, Shacham-Waters 2008) allows a verifier to challenge a storage system with random queries. The storage system responds with proofs that specific blocks exist and are correct. If the proofs verify, the entire dataset is retrievable with high probability.

## DESIGN
- Challenge: verifier sends random block indices + random coefficients
- Response: storage computes linear combination of challenged blocks weighted by coefficients
- Verification: verifier checks response against precomputed authenticators
- Authenticators: computed once at file creation, stored alongside data

## APPLICABILITY
- Future feature: useful for cloud/remote storage verification
- Complementary to RaptorQ: PoR proves data exists, RaptorQ repairs it if it doesn't
- Useful for auditing: periodic PoR challenges prove filesystem integrity without full scan

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] PoR authenticator generation at file creation
- [ ] Challenge-response protocol implemented
- [ ] Verification: false positive rate < 2^-128
- [ ] Benchmark: challenge/response time for 1GB file","acceptance_criteria":"PoR authenticator generation at file creation time. Add integration tests for cross-crate interactions that are currently under-tested.

## Scope

1. **ffs-core ↔ ffs-repair** — End-to-end repair cycle through core orchestration layer. Corrupt → detect → repair → verify.
2. **ffs-mvcc ↔ ffs-journal** — WAL-backed MVCC with journal replay after crash. Verify MVCC state recovers correctly.
3. **ffs-block ↔ ffs-alloc** — Allocation through block cache. Verify allocated blocks are readable via block cache.
4. **ffs-dir ↔ ffs-inode ↔ ffs-extent** — Create file via directory, allocate inode, allocate extents, write data, verify round-trip.

+import sys
+import re
+
+def process_file(path):
+    with open(path, 'r') as f:
+        content = f.read()
+
+    # 1. Remove RequestScope from read_dir, read_inode_attr, read, write, lookup, getattr, readdir, readlink
+    # Match `.function(arg1, &RequestScope::empty(),` or `.function(arg1, &mut RequestScope::empty(),`
+    # Also handle newlines between arguments.
+    # We can use a simpler approach: replace `, &RequestScope::empty()` with empty string if it's the second arg.
+    # Actually, let's use regex with DOTALL or just simple string replacements.
+    
+    # We can just match `.func(\n    cx,\n    &RequestScope::empty(),` etc.
+    # Let's use re.sub with robust patterns.
+    
+    # Remove:
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(read_dir|read_inode_attr|read|write|lookup|getattr|readdir|readlink)\s*\(\s*([^,]+?)\s*,\s*&(?:mut\s*)?RequestScope::empty\(\)\s*(,|\))',
+        r'\1(\2\3',
+        content
+    )
+    
+    # Sometimes it is called with `cx` instead of `&cx`. `([^,]+?)` matches the first argument.
+
+    # 2. Add &RequestScope::empty() as second argument for resolve_extent, read_file_data, read_file, resolve_path
+    # But only if it's not already there. First arg is usually `&cx` or `cx`.
+    # Let's match `\b(resolve_extent|read_file_data|read_file|resolve_path)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,` 
+    # where the next thing is not `&RequestScope` or `scope`.
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(resolve_extent|read_file_data|read_file|resolve_path)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,\s*(?!&?RequestScope|scope)([^)]+)\)',
+        r'\1(\2, &RequestScope::empty(), \3)',
+        content
+    )
+    
+    # 3. Add &mut RequestScope::empty() as second argument for fsync, fsyncdir, flush, setxattr, removexattr, link, symlink, setattr, mkdir
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(fsync|fsyncdir|flush|setxattr|removexattr|link|symlink|setattr|mkdir)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,\s*(?!&?mut RequestScope|scope|&?RequestScope)([^)]+)\)',
+        r'\1(\2, &mut RequestScope::empty(), \3)',
+        content
+    )
+    
+    # Additional cases where parameters are spread across multiple lines:
+    # e.g., 
+    # .getattr(
+    #     &cx,
+    #     &mut RequestScope::empty(),
+    #     InodeNumber(1)
+    # )
+    
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(read_dir|read_inode_attr|read|write|lookup|getattr|readdir|readlink)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,\s*&(?:mut\s*)?RequestScope::empty\(\)\s*,',
+        r'\1(\2,',
+        content,
+        flags=re.MULTILINE
+    )
+    
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(resolve_extent|read_file_data|read_file|resolve_path)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,\s*(?!&?RequestScope|scope)',
+        r'\1(\2, &RequestScope::empty(), ',
+        content,
+        flags=re.MULTILINE
+    )
+    
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'\b(fsync|fsyncdir|flush|setxattr|removexattr|link|symlink|setattr|mkdir)\s*\(\s*(&?cx)\s*,\s*(?!&?mut RequestScope|scope|&?RequestScope)',
+        r'\1(\2, &mut RequestScope::empty(), ',
+        content,
+        flags=re.MULTILINE
+    )
+    
+    with open(path, 'w') as f:
+        f.write(content)
+
+process_file('crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs')
+process_file('crates/ffs-core/src/vfs.rs')
+process_file('crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs')
+print("Done")

+import os
+import re
+
+def fix_file(filepath):
+    if not os.path.exists(filepath):
+        return
+    with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
+        content = f.read()
+
+    # fallocate with libc:: constants
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'(\.fallocate\([^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*)(libc::[A-Z_]+(?:\s*\|\s*libc::[A-Z_]+)*)(\s*\))',
+        r'\1(\2).try_into().unwrap()\3',
+        content
+    )
+
+    # read with u32::try_from -> needs u64::from
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'(\.read\([^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*)(u32::try_from\([^)]+\)\.unwrap\(\))',
+        r'\1u64::from(\2)',
+        content
+    )
+    
+    # read with read_len -> needs u64::from(read_len)
+    content = re.sub(
+        r'(\.read\([^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*[^,]+,\s*)(read_len)(\s*\))',
+        r'\1u64::from(\2)\3',
+        content
+    )
+
+    with open(filepath, 'w') as f:
+        f.write(content)
+
+fix_file('crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs')
+print("Fixes applied.")
